LC #11 — Container With Most Water medium

Two Pointers (Greedy) Pattern  |  Day 9 Study Material

📋 Problem + Core Insight

🎯 LeetCode 11: Container With Most Water

The Core Insight: Width decreases as pointers move in. The only way

to increase area is to find a taller line. So always move the shorter pointer inward.

📋 Problem

Given integer array height of length n, there are n vertical lines drawn such that the two endpoints of the ith line are (i, 0) and (i, height[i]).

Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container holding the most water.

height = [1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7]
→ 49
(Lines at index 1 (height 8) and index 8 (height 7). Area = min(8,7) * (8-1) = 7 * 7 = 49)

Constraints: 2 ≤ n ≤ 10⁵ | 0 ≤ height[i] ≤ 10⁴

🧠 Mental Models

🧠 Choose Your Mental Model

Model The "Story" Mechanism
The Greedy Funnel "I start with the widest possible bucket. If I shrink the width, I MUST find a taller wall to compensate. I drop the shortest wall because it limits my capacity." Pointers inward, drop min
Max Envelope "The area is bounded by the smallest limit. Squeezing the boundaries only makes sense if the limit increases." Two Ends Converge

📊 Complexity Analysis

🔬 Complexity Analysis: Brute Force vs Optimal

The Core Insight: A naive approach evaluates every combination of two lines O(n²).

Using a greedy two-pointer contraction shrinks the search space mathematically since we safely discard suboptimal pairs, leading to O(n) time.

Approach Time Space Notes
Brute force O(n²) O(1) Nested loops over all pairs
Two Pointers O(n) O(1) One single pass inward

🚀 Optimal Solution

def maxArea(height: list[int]) -> int:
    left, right = 0, len(height) - 1
    max_water = 0

    while left < right:
        width = right - left
        current = min(height[left], height[right]) * width
        max_water = max(max_water, current)

        # Move the shorter side — the taller side can't help with a smaller width
        if height[left] <= height[right]:
            left += 1
        else:
            right -= 1

    return max_water

🎤 Interview Q&A

🎤 5 Interview Q&A

Q1: Why move the shorter pointer?

Answer: Area = min(left_height, right_height) × width. When we move inward, width decreases by 1.

The only hope of increasing the area is finding a taller line.

Moving the taller line guarantees we keep or decrease the height — no upside.

Moving the shorter line gives a chance to find something taller.

Q2: Is this provably optimal?

Answer: Yes — by induction. If we move the shorter pointer, we've already computed the

best area possible for that shorter line at any position (since any position to the right has ≤ width).

Therefore, we can safely eliminate the shorter line without missing a valid maximum answer.

Q3: What happens if both heights are exactly equal?

Answer: You can safely move either pointer (or even both). Since the capacity is bottlenecked by both

equal heights, finding a taller line on just one side won't increase the area because the

opposite equal-height line will remain the bottleneck while the width shrinks.

Q4: What if there is a massive spike in the middle of the array?

Answer: The two pointers will naturally converge over it. Since they constantly discard the smaller outer walls,

they will climb inward until they both stand on the massive spikes (if there are two), extracting the maximum valid area.

Q5: Can this be solved using Divide and Conquer?

Answer: Technically yes, but it would involve evaluating cross-boundary containers which is complex and

usually bounds out at O(n log n). The greedy two-pointer strategy at O(n) is the strictly superior approach for this problem.

💼 The Citi Angle

💼 The Citi Narrative

Context: In liquidity management and dark pool trading operations, Citi must match

buyers and sellers across different time horizons to maximize the volume bounds of executed trades.

The Problem: "Evaluate trade volume thresholds across a time series to find the maximum possible matched liquidity capacity."

How Greedy Contraction Applied: If you consider the distance between the transaction timestamps

as the "width" and the available liquidity volume as the "height", finding the optimal cross trade is identical to the

Most Water problem. Instead of O(n²) exhaustive evaluation of every historical tick pair, the

APM infrastructure can do a two-pointer sliding bounds check across the day's liquidity histogram to find optimal matching horizons in linear time.

Interview Line: *"At Citi, matching dark pool liquidity over time limits is essentially a capacity bounds problem.

Instead of doing a highly inefficient O(n²) cross-correlation between every tick, we treated the

liquidity histograms as limiting bounds and used a greedy two-pointer contraction from the daily open and close.

By discarding the smaller liquidity constraints inwards, we identified the maximum trade duration capacities in O(1) space."*

"Simplicity and clarity is Gold." — Sean's Study Mantra 🚀
